name: American Museum of Natural History (Rose Center), New York
concept&appearance: Designed by James Polshek and Todd Schliemann of Polshek Partnership (now Ennead Architects), the Rose Center is conceived as a 'Cosmic Cathedral'. Its iconic architectural form consists of an 87-foot (27 m) diameter sphere (housing the Hayden Planetarium) housed within a 95-foot high transparent glass cube. The sphere appears to float freely within the cube, supported by a truss system, symbolizing the Sun or a celestial body suspended in the vastness of space (represented by the transparent volume). The building features a high-clarity 'water-white' glass curtain wall held by a tension-truss system to maximize transparency and demystify science. The design completes the museum's north side and includes the Arthur Ross Terrace, a public space built over a parking garage.
permanent_exhibitions:
1. Harriet and Robert Heilbrunn Cosmic Pathway: A 360-foot-long spiral ramp that winds around the sphere, representing a timeline of the universe's 13-billion-year history. Visitors walk down the path where each step equals 75 million years, passing markers for cosmic events like the formation of the Milky Way and the extinction of dinosaurs.
2. Scales of the Universe: Located on a 400-foot-long walkway along the glass curtain wall, this exhibit uses the massive Hayden Sphere as a reference point to illustrate the relative sizes of objects in the cosmos, ranging from subatomic particles to galaxies, using powers of ten.
3. Dorothy and Lewis B. Cullman Hall of the Universe: Situated on the lower level beneath the sphere, this hall is divided into four zones (Universe, Galaxies, Stars, Planets). It features the Willamette Meteorite, interactive screens, and exhibits on modern astrophysics, such as black holes and dark matter.
4. David S. and Ruth L. Gottesman Hall of Planet Earth: A dedicated hall exploring Earth's geological history and systems. It features 168 rock specimens from around the world, including black smokers from the ocean floor and ancient banded iron formations, organized to explain plate tectonics, climate change, and rock cycles.
central_hall: The 'central' volume of the Rose Center is the massive, sun-filled glass atrium created by the cube structure itself. The 87-foot Hayden Sphere dominates this space, hanging above the lower exhibition level (Cullman Hall of the Universe). The floor of the atrium (Level 1) provides access to the Cosmic Pathway and offers views of the sphere's underside and the surrounding glass walls.
special_effects_theaters:
1. Hayden Planetarium (Space Theater): Located in the top half of the Hayden Sphere. It seats 429 people and features a 68-foot diameter dome screen. It utilizes a Zeiss Mark IX Star Projector for accurate night sky replication and a digital projection system (currently Christie Eclipse 4K HDR RGB laser projectors) to visualize 3D astrophysical data.
2. Big Bang Theater: Located in the bottom half of the Hayden Sphere. It uses a screen measuring 36 feet in diameter set over an 8-foot-deep bowl. A 4-minute multi-sensory presentation, narrated by Liam Neeson, simulates the Big Bang and the origin of the universe using lasers and lighting effects.
science_popularization_activities: The Rose Center serves as a major hub for science education and research. It hosts the 'Astronomy Live' series, public lectures (such as the Frontiers of Astrophysics), and school field trip programs. The Department of Astrophysics is housed within the facility, bridging research with public outreach. Regular 'Space Shows' (e.g., 'Worlds Beyond Earth', 'Dark Universe') are produced using scientific visualization to educate the public on complex cosmic topics.
